title-group.article-title: Factors affecting voriconazole pharmacokinetic variability in critically ill patients: a systematic review

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

body.p: We thank Langbeen and colleagues for their interest in our systematic review and for raising the issue of a potential interaction between voriconazole and isoxazolyl penicillins [1]. Such discussions are clinically relevant, given the narrow therapeutic window of voriconazole and the high potential of drug-drug interaction involving cytochrome P450 (CYP) enzymes, including in critically ill patients. As noted by the authors, our systematic review included longitudinal and before–after designs when available (for example, for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation) but no ICU-specific datasets fulfilling our inclusion criteria were available for this specific interaction [2]. As also mentioned by Langbeen et al., cefazolin is increasingly used as an alternative to isoxazolyl penicillins for the treatment of methicillin-susceptible Staphylococcus aureus infections, a strategy supported by recent randomized evidence [3, 4].

We agree that enzymatic induction by isoxazolyl penicillins is plausible. Induction of CYP2C19, CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 has been demonstrated with dicloxacillin in healthy volunteers, supporting a potential class effect [5]. However, the magnitude and clinical relevance of this interaction remain uncertain in critically ill patients. The available clinical evidence involving flucloxacillin is from observational datasets, including the study by Van Daele et al. The analysis based on before–after comparisons did not account for key time-varying covariables such as systemic inflammation. Because inflammation is known to suppress CYP activity, its resolution during effective antibiotic therapy may increase metabolic clearance and mimic an induction-like effect if not adjusted for [6].

This leads us more broadly to consider a phenomenon that is not yet fully understood, known as metabolic phenoconversion [7]. Phenoconversion refers to a mismatch between the genetically predicted metabolic phenotype and the observed phenotype, due to environmental factors such as drug interactions, inflammation, or comorbidities. Indeed, the main determinant of voriconazole pharmacokinetic variability is its metabolism, which is genetically determined. International guidelines provide recommendations for voriconazole dosing or alternative therapy based on genotype [8]. Voriconazole is predominantly metabolized by CYP2C19 and, to a lesser extent, by CYP3A4 and CYP2C9—enzymes whose activity, particularly that of CYP2C19, depends on genetic polymorphisms. Currently, two loss-of-function alleles (CYP2C19*2 and CYP2C19*3) and one gain-of-function allele (CYP2C19*17) have been well described; in populations of European ancestry, the most common loss-of-function allele CYP2C19*2 has an allele frequency of approximately 15%, corresponding to heterozygous and/or homozygous carriage, whereas the gain-of-function allele CYP2C19*17 occurs with a frequency of about 20–25%. Depending on the activity level of the different alleles, five CYP2C19 phenotypes are classically defined: poor metabolizer (PM), intermediate metabolizer (IM), normal metabolizer (NM), rapid metabolizer (RM), and ultrarapid metabolizer (UM) [9].

Drug–drug interactions and several clinical conditions may induce phenoconversion [8]. For example, inhibitors of CYP2C19 may lead to phenoconversion toward a PM phenotype, including proton pump inhibitors and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors uch as fluvoxamine. It has also been well described in the setting of voriconazole therapy during systemic inflammation [4]. Other situations including advanced age [9], liver diseases, and advanced cancer [10] could conduct to similar phenotypes. Interestingly, most of these conditions are likely to be common among ICU patients treated with voriconazole.

On the other hand, CYP2C19 inducers may lead to phenoconversion toward a rapid (RM) or ultrarapid (UM) metabolizer phenotype, including carbamazepine, rifampicin, St John’s wort, and, as mentioned above, possibly antistaphylococcal penicillins. Some other drugs have not been specifically studied from a phenoconversion perspective but are known CYP3A4 inducers, such as high-dose dexamethasone. However, conditions associated with phenoconversion toward a RM or UM phenotype seem relatively uncommon.

The systematic review included some of these known factors of phenoconversion, including steroids, proton pump inhibitors, and hepatic impairment [2]. However, determining the metabolic phenotype based on patient characteristics, gentotype and co-medications is quite complex and likely to vary over time. Such complexity may not be adequately captured a priori approaches, even when genotype is included [10]. Repeated therapeutic drug monitoring, combined with pharmacokinetic models using a posteriori predictions, may therefore represent a more appropriate strategy in this setting.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: A.C. and M.G. wrote the main manuscript text. All authors reviewed the manuscript and contributed substantial revisions. All the authors read and approved the final manuscript.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
